if you’re asking which is “wrong” and which is “right” then you’re completely misunderstanding the point of models. They are all projections based on probabilities and at this point, none of them are likely to hit.
For example, ESPN now has computer models that say the KC chiefs are a 98% probability to make the playoffs.Now I’m not sure what goes into their model, but I’m sure some of their model is accounting for the fact they are 4-0, defending champs, and have a solid roster. But if Pat Mahomes slips in the shower, breaks his leg and they miss the playoffs, it won’t be because the model is “wrong”. It will be because the model couldn’t reasonably account for such an injury.
All these are is a snapshot prediction based on probability at a given time. Most will not pan out. When they don’t, the question isn’t “why is this model bad,” but rather “what variable, which this model didn’t fully account for, needs to be worked into future models?” …or in the Mahomes example “ what data can these models never accurately predict”. Sometimes the wind 5 days out falls into the latter category
